ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general lettuce food product entry; no specific entry for Cantonese-style blanched iceberg lettuce with oyster sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers lettuce and salad plants broadly; no specific code for blanched preparation with oyster sauce. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for this prepared vegetable dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of blanched iceberg lettuce is approximately 200 g, comprising a full head of lettuce leaves blanched and plated with oyster sauce and garlic oil.

nutrient_source.title: Iceberg (includes crisphead) lettuce, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #11252; adapted with oyster sauce contribution estimated from USDA FoodData Central #170493
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values from USDA SR Legacy iceberg lettuce raw (FDC #11252). Sodium and minor macro adjustments made to reflect typical oyster sauce and garlic oil dressing contribution as served in Cantonese-style restaurants. Oyster sauce reference: USDA FDC #170493. Values represent the composite dish per 100 g as plated.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of vegetables — lettuce and non-starchy leafy vegetables; analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for blanched iceberg lettuce with oyster sauce. GI estimated at 15 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-starchy leafy vegetables as very low GI (GI range 10–20). Iceberg lettuce is a non-starchy vegetable with minimal digestible carbohydrate. GL per 200 g serving = (15 × 5.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.84, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.guidance: The oyster sauce and soy sauce dressing contributes approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce or limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake below 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and minimal digestible carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 15 and only 2.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g, blanched iceberg lettuce has negligible impact on postprandial blood glucose and is suitable for inclusion in a Type 2 Diabetes meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high water content, and vegetable micronutrients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and provides folate and potassium; the primary cardiovascular consideration is the sodium content from oyster sauce, which should be moderated in individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Blanched Iceberg Lettuce (白灼西生菜, Bai Zhuo Xi Sheng Cai) is a classic Cantonese vegetable dish widely served in Chinese restaurants and Hong Kong-style bing sutt (ice rooms) across Singapore and southern China. The dish is prepared by briefly blanching whole or halved iceberg lettuce leaves in boiling water or stock for 30–60 seconds until just wilted but still retaining a crisp texture, then draining and plating. The lettuce is finished with a drizzle of hot oyster sauce, light soy sauce, and fragrant garlic-infused oil, sometimes garnished with fried shallots or sesame oil. The result is a dish with a mild, slightly sweet flavour, a tender-crisp texture, and a savoury umami sauce coating. Nutritionally, the dish is very low in calories (approximately 28 kcal per 100 g), low in carbohydrate (2.8 g per 100 g), and provides dietary fibre, folate, potassium, and vitamin A. The primary nutritional concern is sodium from the oyster sauce dressing, which can reach 280 mg per 100 g. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 15), making it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. It is a popular side dish in Cantonese cuisine, frequently ordered alongside rice dishes, roasted meats, and noodles. In Singapore, it appears on menus at zi char stalls, Hong Kong-style cafes, and dim sum restaurants.
common_user_queries: What is bai zhuo xi sheng cai?
How many calories in blanched lettuce with oyster sauce?
Is 白灼西生菜 suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of blanched lettuce?
How is Cantonese blanched lettuce prepared?
Is blanched lettuce high in sodium?
What sauce is used on blanched lettuce in Chinese restaurants?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry found for this specific prepared dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy iceberg lettuce raw data with estimated sauce contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database non-starchy vegetable category. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy iceberg lettuce raw (FDC #11252) with sodium and fat adjusted upward to reflect typical oyster sauce and garlic oil dressing contribution as served in Cantonese restaurants. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — iceberg lettuce and oyster sauce contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — this is a plant-based vegetable dish with no animal-derived B12 source in the base vegetable; trace amounts may enter via oyster sauce but are below detection threshold and reported as 0 in USDA source). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — iceberg lettuce contains no vitamin D; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — iceberg lettuce contains no cholesterol; oyster sauce contributes negligible cholesterol below rounding threshold). monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy data and are plausible for this dish. GI set to 15 as analogous estimate for non-starchy leafy vegetable per University of Sydney GI Database category guidance. Dish is not halal-certified by default as oyster sauce contains mollusc derivatives; halal status depends on restaurant certification. Allergen note: oyster sauce contains molluscs and soy; soy sauce contains gluten.
